Week 5 Workshop Walkthrough

1 The Plan

In this session I’ll lead you through the steps of completing an assignment, similar to your 7CS034 assignment, using a different dataset. I’d like you to follow along with me today and produce the same report that I do. Do not work on you module assessment in this session, we won’t help you with that. But, we will help you with producing the report which we are all working on today.

I’ve created a starter file for you and I’ve set some YAML settings at the top of the starter file to make it behave the way that I like - feel free to modify the settings yourself to make it behave the way you like it.

2 Get the starter files and dataset

On your desktop create a new folder called Week 5. Then downoad the following files from canvas and save them inside it.

  • data.csv the dataset

  • 01843621.qmd my starter file named using the convention outlined in the assessment brief

Launch RStudio if you haven’t done so already.

2.1 About the Dataset

For the purpose of today’s workshop we are working with the famous Wisconsin Breast Cancer Dataset. You can find a full description of the dataset at the above link on the UC Irvine, Machine Learning Repository which is a great source of datasets for AI and Data Science generally.

Load the necessary packages and libraries first

Code 
library(tidyverse)
── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.3     ✔ readr     2.1.4
✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.0
✔ ggplot2   3.4.4     ✔ tibble    3.2.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.3     ✔ tidyr     1.3.0
✔ purrr     1.0.2     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
Code 
library(ggplot2)
library(ggthemes)

Read the dataset into Rstudio as normal

Code 
data <- read_csv("data.csv")
Rows: 569 Columns: 32
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr  (1): diagnosis
dbl (31): id, radius_mean, texture_mean, perimeter_mean, area_mean, smoothne...

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

Note that you’ll need to specify the correct path to where you have saved the data file in order to read the data with read_csv().

Next let’s take a peek at the data to see what we are dealing with.

Code 
View(data)

The above command let’s us view the dataset within RStudio. We can also do

Code 
head(data)
# A tibble: 6 × 32
        id diagnosis radius_mean texture_mean perimeter_mean area_mean
     <dbl> <chr>           <dbl>        <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
1   842302 M                18.0         10.4          123.      1001 
2   842517 M                20.6         17.8          133.      1326 
3 84300903 M                19.7         21.2          130       1203 
4 84348301 M                11.4         20.4           77.6      386.
5 84358402 M                20.3         14.3          135.      1297 
6   843786 M                12.4         15.7           82.6      477.
# ℹ 26 more variables: smoothness_mean <dbl>, compactness_mean <dbl>,
#   concavity_mean <dbl>, `concave points_mean` <dbl>, symmetry_mean <dbl>,
#   fractal_dimension_mean <dbl>, radius_se <dbl>, texture_se <dbl>,
#   perimeter_se <dbl>, area_se <dbl>, smoothness_se <dbl>,
#   compactness_se <dbl>, concavity_se <dbl>, `concave points_se` <dbl>,
#   symmetry_se <dbl>, fractal_dimension_se <dbl>, radius_worst <dbl>,
#   texture_worst <dbl>, perimeter_worst <dbl>, area_worst <dbl>, …

to check the types of the variables. We can see that diagnosis is a variable of type chr while the rest are of type double. This makes sense since diagnosis can take the values

  • B = benign

  • M = malignant

In fact, this dataset has been produced to serve as a tool for using the measurements in the other columns to classify cells as cancerous or not. In this sense, there is something immediately of interest here, namely: what is the relationship between the various measurements in the other columns and whether or not a cell is classified as benign or malignant? A partial answer to this question could well be the subject of the piece of work we produce today.

3 Our Coursework

Now we’ll start working directly on the mock coursework we will prepare today. We’ll work on the introdcution for the next few minutes.

3.1 Introduction

General introductory remarks about what we are doing and some references as required.

3.2 The Data

We don’t need to give an extensive piece here about the data but we should certainly write something, which we can change/amend later as necessary.

3.3 Investigation

This is where we’ll spend most of our time but we’ll only include the interesting stuff in our final report. The first thing I decided to do was to look at possible relationships between the variable in the dataset. One such tool is a correlation plot which we can create using the corrplot package.

Code 
library(corrplot)
corrplot 0.92 loaded
Code 
M<-data[3:32]
c<-cor(M)
corrplot(c)

There is a lot to see here and now we need to think about what this means.

  • It might be interesting to create correlation plots only for the benign and malignant cases.

  • Can you do that?

  • Does that help you to know where to look for something interesting?

3.4 When we find something

Once we find something interesting we can plot it and offer an explanation.
